note.soap:
S: 51-year-old patient. The patient reports sinus pain lasting 6 days without severe pain or fever. The symptoms have not worsened after initial improvement, and there is no eye swelling. The patient has a known penicillin allergy. Pregnancy status is unknown. No systemic symptoms reported.

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Vital signs and physical findings are not available. No signs of orbital or neurological involvement reported.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute bacterial sinusitis  
  - Differential: Viral sinusitis, allergic rhinosinusitis, dental infection  
  - Red flags considered: Orbital swelling, neurological symptoms, severe pain or fever, worsening symptoms after initial improvement—all absent.

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage in urgent care or primary care setting with close follow-up.  
  - Plan: Recommend symptomatic treatment with analgesics and nasal saline irrigation. Given penicillin allergy, if antibiotics are indicated due to persistent symptoms beyond 10 days or worsening, consider doxycycline or a respiratory fluoroquinolone pending physician review. Advise patient to monitor symptoms closely.  
  - Safety net: Instruct patient to seek urgent care if symptoms worsen, develop fever, facial swelling, vision changes, or neurological symptoms.

Evidence (snippets used):  
- Duration: 6 days  
- Severe pain or fever: no  
- Worse after better: no  
- Eye swelling: no  
- Penicillin allergy present  
- Pregnancy status is unknown
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
